items.title: API server not public
items.description: Restrict kube-apiserver to private networks or authorized IPs.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether the API server endpoint is reachable from the internet. For managed clusters inspect the endpoint config; from a host, probe the endpoint:

```
kubectl cluster-info
curl -k https://<api-server>:6443/version
```

For EKS/GKE/AKS confirm the endpoint is private or IP-restricted (e.g. `aws eks describe-cluster --name <c> --query 'cluster.resourcesVpcConfig.[endpointPublicAccess,publicAccessCidrs]'`). A `0.0.0.0/0` reachable `:6443` is the finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Make the control-plane endpoint private or restrict it to admin CIDRs / a bastion. On EKS:

```
aws eks update-cluster-config --name <c> --resources-vpc-config endpointPublicAccess=false,endpointPrivateAccess=true
```

Front admin access with a VPN/bastion and enforce mTLS. Re-verify the endpoint is no longer publicly reachable.

items.title: RBAC least privilege
items.description: No cluster-admin for apps; namespace-scoped roles only.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate who can do what and hunt for over-broad grants:

```
kubectl get clusterrolebindings,rolebindings -A -o wide
kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<sa>
```

Wildcard rules (`verbs: ["*"]`, `resources: ["*"]`) or many subjects bound to `cluster-admin` are findings. `kubectl-who-can` and `rbac-tool` help audit at scale.
items.how_to_fix:
Replace wildcard roles with least-privilege Roles scoped per namespace, bind to specific service accounts, and remove unused `cluster-admin` bindings:

```
kubectl delete clusterrolebinding <over-broad-binding>
kubectl create role app-ro --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods -n <ns>
kubectl create rolebinding app-ro --role=app-ro --serviceaccount=<ns>:<sa> -n <ns>
```

Review quarterly with `rbac-tool` and enforce via policy in CI.

items.title: Pod Security Standards enforced
items.description: Restricted or baseline policy on all namespaces.
items.how_to_find:
Check namespaces for Pod Security Admission labels:

```
kubectl get ns -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.metadata.labels.pod-security\.kubernetes\.io/enforce}{"\n"}{end}'
```

Namespaces without `enforce=restricted` (or `baseline`) allow privileged pods and are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Label namespaces to enforce the restricted profile:

```
kubectl label ns <ns> pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version=latest --overwrite
```

Roll out with `warn`/`audit` first, fix violations, then flip to `enforce`. Deploy labels via GitOps so they can't drift.

items.title: Secrets not in env vars
items.description: Use CSI drivers or mounted volumes; encrypt etcd at rest.
items.how_to_find:
Find pods exposing secrets through environment variables (visible in specs, logs, crash dumps):

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.containers[].env[]?.valueFrom.secretKeyRef) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```

Any `secretKeyRef` under `env` is a finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Mount secrets as files (tmpfs volumes) or inject via a CSI secrets driver instead of env vars:

```
volumes: [{name: sec, csi: {driver: secrets-store.csi.k8s.io, readOnly: true, volumeAttributes: {secretProviderClass: <spc>}}}]
```

Migrate to an external manager (Vault/ASM/GSM) via the Secrets Store CSI driver and rotate any previously-exposed secrets.

items.title: Network policies default deny
items.description: Explicit allow rules between pods and egress.
items.how_to_find:
List namespaces that have no NetworkPolicy (all pod traffic allowed):

```
for ns in $(kubectl get ns -o name | cut -d/ -f2); do echo "$ns: $(kubectl get netpol -n $ns --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l)"; done
```

`0` policies in a workload namespace = finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Apply a default-deny ingress+egress policy per namespace, then allow only required flows:

```
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata: {name: default-deny, namespace: <ns>}
spec: {podSelector: {}, policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]}
EOF
```

Confirm your CNI (Calico/Cilium) enforces policy, then add explicit allow rules.

items.title: Images scanned for CVEs
items.description: Trivy or similar in CI; block critical vulns on deploy.
items.how_to_find:
Scan images used in the cluster for known CVEs:

```
trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL <registry>/<image>:<tag>
grype <registry>/<image>:<tag>
```

Images with fixable HIGH/CRITICAL CVEs are findings. Pull the live image list with `kubectl get pods -A -o jsonpath='{..image}' | tr ' ' '\n' | sort -u`.
items.how_to_fix:
Gate CVE scanning in CI and block on fixable HIGH/CRITICAL:

```
trivy image --exit-code 1 --ignore-unfixed --severity HIGH,CRITICAL <image>
```

Rebuild on updated base images, patch dependencies, and re-scan images already running with an in-cluster scanner (Trivy Operator).

items.title: No privileged containers
items.description: A privileged container effectively has root on the host node, disabling most isolation and providing a direct path to node compromise.
items.how_to_find:
Find privileged containers cluster-wide:

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged==true) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```

Any `privileged: true` container is a critical finding (full node access).
items.how_to_fix:
Remove `privileged: true`, grant only the specific capabilities needed, and block it at admission:

```
securityContext: {privileged: false, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities: {drop: ["ALL"]}}
```

Enforce with Pod Security Admission `restricted` or a Kyverno/Gatekeeper policy that denies privileged pods.

items.title: Docker socket not mounted
items.description: Never mount /var/run/docker.sock into application containers.
items.how_to_find:
Find pods mounting the container engine socket (a direct node-takeover path):

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.volumes[]?.hostPath.path | test("docker.sock|containerd.sock|crio.sock")?) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove the `hostPath` socket mount. For CI/build workloads use rootless builders (Kaniko, Buildah, BuildKit) instead of the daemon socket. Block socket `hostPath` mounts via a Kyverno/Gatekeeper policy.

items.title: Image signing and admission control
items.description: Only signed images from trusted registries.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether unsigned images can be admitted. Inspect for a signature-verification policy (Kyverno `verifyImages` / Sigstore policy-controller) and test:

```
kubectl get clusterpolicies 2>/dev/null
cosign verify --key <key> <registry>/<image>:<tag>
```

No verification policy = finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Sign images in CI and enforce verification at admission:

```
cosign sign --key <key> <registry>/<image>@<digest>
```

Deploy Kyverno `verifyImages` (or Sigstore policy-controller) to deny unsigned images, keyed to your trusted keys/Fulcio identities.

items.title: Resource limits set
items.description: CPU and memory limits prevent DoS via resource exhaustion.
items.how_to_find:
Find containers without CPU/memory requests+limits (DoS / noisy-neighbor risk):

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.containers[] | (.resources.limits==null)) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix:
Set requests+limits on every container and enforce with a LimitRange + ResourceQuota per namespace:

```
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata: {name: defaults, namespace: <ns>}
spec: {limits: [{type: Container, default: {cpu: 500m, memory: 512Mi}, defaultRequest: {cpu: 100m, memory: 128Mi}}]}
EOF
```

Deny limitless pods at admission via policy.

items.title: Use minimal, trusted base images
items.description: Bloated base images (full OS distros) drastically expand the attack surface with shells, package managers, and unneeded binaries that aid post-exploitation.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect base images and layer count/size; large general-purpose bases (full `ubuntu`/`debian`) expand attack surface:

```
docker history --no-trunc <image>:<tag>
trivy image --scanners vuln <image>:<tag>
```

Images built on non-minimal bases with many OS packages are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Rebuild on distroless/minimal bases (e.g. `gcr.io/distroless/static`, `alpine`) from trusted publishers, pinned by digest:

```
FROM gcr.io/distroless/base-debian12@sha256:<digest>
```

Prefer multi-stage builds so only the runtime artifact ships. Re-scan to confirm reduced CVE count.

items.title: No secrets baked into image layers
items.description: Secrets copied or referenced during a build persist in intermediate layers even if deleted in a later layer, and can be extracted from the image history.
items.how_to_find:
Scan image layers/history for embedded secrets:

```
trivy image --scanners secret <image>:<tag>
docker history --no-trunc <image>:<tag>
```

Any API key, token, private key, or `.env` in a layer is a finding (layers persist even if deleted later).
items.how_to_fix:
Remove secrets from Dockerfiles/build args; use BuildKit secret mounts and multi-stage builds so nothing is baked in:

```
RUN --mount=type=secret,id=npmtoken npm ci
```

Rotate any leaked secret immediately and add `trivy --scanners secret` (and gitleaks) to CI.

items.title: Pin images to immutable digests, not mutable tags
items.description: Using mutable tags like :latest means the running image can silently change, breaking reproducibility and enabling tag-overwrite supply-chain attacks.
items.how_to_find:
Find workloads referencing mutable tags (`:latest`, `:v1`) instead of `@sha256:` digests:

```
kubectl get pods -A -o jsonpath='{range .items[*].spec.containers[*]}{.image}{"\n"}{end}' | grep -v '@sha256:'
```
items.how_to_fix:
Pin images by digest in manifests and set `imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent` with immutable references:

```
image: <registry>/<image>@sha256:<digest>
```

Resolve tags to digests in CI, and enable registry tag immutability so tags can't be overwritten.

items.title: Strip build tooling and dev dependencies from runtime image
items.description: Leaving compilers, package managers, and debugging utilities in the final image gives an attacker ready-made tools to escalate and pivot after a foothold.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether the runtime image contains compilers, package managers, shells, or dev packages:

```
trivy image <image>:<tag> | grep -Ei 'gcc|make|npm|pip|apt|curl|wget'
docker run --rm --entrypoint sh <image>:<tag> -c 'which sh bash curl wget gcc || true'
```
items.how_to_fix:
Use multi-stage builds: compile in a builder stage, copy only the artifact into a distroless/scratch runtime stage:

```
FROM golang:1.22 AS build
...
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static
COPY --from=build /app /app
```

This removes shells/tooling attackers rely on. Re-scan to confirm.

sections.title: Image & Registry Security — Vulnerability Scanning & SBOM

items.title: Scan images for known vulnerabilities (CVEs)
items.description: Images frequently ship with OS packages and language dependencies carrying known, exploitable CVEs that are never patched without scanning.
items.how_to_find:
Run a scanner against each image and fail on fixable high-severity issues:

```
trivy image --ignore-unfixed --severity HIGH,CRITICAL <image>:<tag>
grype <image>:<tag> -o table
```
items.how_to_fix:
Integrate scanning into CI (block on fixable HIGH/CRITICAL) and continuously in-cluster:

```
trivy image --exit-code 1 --ignore-unfixed --severity HIGH,CRITICAL <image>
```

Deploy the Trivy Operator to re-scan running workloads and rebuild on patched bases.

items.title: Generate and retain an SBOM per image
items.description: A software bill of materials enables rapid impact assessment when a new CVE drops (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether an SBOM is produced and stored per image/release:

```
syft <image>:<tag> -o spdx-json > sbom.spdx.json
cosign download sbom <image>:<tag>
```

No SBOM = finding (can't answer "am I affected by CVE-X?").
items.how_to_fix:
Generate an SBOM in CI, attach it to the image as an attestation, and retain it:

```
syft <image>@<digest> -o cyclonedx-json > sbom.json
cosign attest --predicate sbom.json --type cyclonedx --key <key> <image>@<digest>
```

Feed SBOMs into your vuln management to enable fast impact analysis.

items.title: Scan images already running in the cluster
items.description: Images that passed scanning at build time accumulate new CVEs over time, and unscanned images may have been deployed out-of-band.
items.how_to_find:
Scan the live image set, not just CI artifacts:

```
kubectl get pods -A -o jsonpath='{..image}' | tr ' ' '\n' | sort -u | while read i; do trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL "$i"; done
```
items.how_to_fix:
Deploy an in-cluster scanner that continuously reports on running workloads:

```
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/trivy-operator/main/deploy/static/trivy-operator.yaml
kubectl get vulnerabilityreports -A
```

Alert on new HIGH/CRITICAL and drive rebuilds.

items.title: Verify dependency provenance and integrity
items.description: Dependencies pulled from public registries can be typosquatted, hijacked, or tampered with, injecting malicious code into images.
items.how_to_find:
Check for build provenance/attestations (SLSA) and verify signatures on dependencies and images:

```
cosign verify-attestation --type slsaprovenance --key <key> <image>@<digest>
```

No provenance / unverifiable dependencies = critical supply-chain finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Produce SLSA provenance in CI, sign artifacts, and verify at admission:

```
cosign attest --predicate provenance.json --type slsaprovenance --key <key> <image>@<digest>
```

Pin dependencies by hash, use lockfiles, and enforce provenance verification via a Kyverno/policy-controller admission policy.

items.title: Require authentication and access control on registries
items.description: Anonymous or weakly protected registries let attackers pull proprietary images or push malicious ones that get deployed.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether the registry allows anonymous pull/push and review access:

```
curl -s https://<registry>/v2/_catalog
curl -s https://<registry>/v2/<repo>/tags/list
```

A catalog/tags response without credentials is a finding.
items.how_to_fix: Require authentication, use per-team robot accounts with least privilege, and disable anonymous access. Front with a hardened registry (Harbor/ECR/GAR) and enforce RBAC + image immutability. Rotate any exposed pull/push credentials.

items.title: Sign images and verify signatures
items.description: Unsigned images cannot be cryptographically attributed to a trusted builder, enabling substitution and supply-chain attacks.
items.how_to_find:
Check that images are signed and signatures verify:

```
cosign verify --key <key> <registry>/<image>@<digest>
```

Unsigned images (or no verification enforced) are critical findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Sign in CI with cosign (keyless/Fulcio or KMS key) and enforce verification at admission:

```
cosign sign --key <key> <registry>/<image>@<digest>
```

Deploy Kyverno `verifyImages` / Sigstore policy-controller to deny unsigned or untrusted-identity images.

items.title: Enforce trusted-registry admission policy
items.description: Without admission control, workloads can pull images from arbitrary external registries, bypassing scanning and signing controls.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether pods can pull from arbitrary registries. Look for an admission policy restricting registries and test by deploying from an untrusted one:

```
kubectl get clusterpolicies,constrainttemplates 2>/dev/null
```

No allowlist = finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Enforce a trusted-registry allowlist at admission (Kyverno example):

```
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata: {name: allowed-registries}
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Enforce
  rules: [{name: r, match: {any: [{resources: {kinds: [Pod]}}]}, validate: {message: "untrusted registry", pattern: {spec: {containers: [{image: "<registry>/*"}]}}}}]
```

Deny anything outside your registries.

items.title: Check for exposed or world-readable registries
items.description: Internal registries exposed to the internet or misconfigured cloud storage backing them can leak entire image catalogs and embedded secrets.
items.how_to_find:
Look for internet-exposed registries and anonymous access (a common breach source):

```
curl -s https://<host>:5000/v2/_catalog
nmap -p 5000,443 --open <host>
```

An enumerable `_catalog` without auth is critical (image tampering + secret exposure).
items.how_to_fix: Put the registry behind auth + TLS, remove public exposure (private subnet / IP allowlist), and enable scanning + immutability. Rotate credentials and audit pull/push logs for tampering. Re-test that `_catalog` requires authentication.

sections.title: Container Runtime Security — Privileged & Host Exposure

items.title: No hostPID / hostIPC / hostNetwork sharing
items.description: Sharing host PID, IPC, or network namespaces lets a container observe and interfere with host processes, IPC, and traffic, undermining isolation.
items.how_to_find:
Find pods sharing host namespaces (enables snooping/escape):

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.hostPID==true or .spec.hostIPC==true or .spec.hostNetwork==true) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove `hostPID`/`hostIPC`/`hostNetwork` from workload specs (only rare node-agents need them). Enforce via Pod Security Admission `restricted` or a policy that denies host-namespace sharing.

items.title: No sensitive hostPath volume mounts
items.description: Mounting host paths such as /, /var/run, /etc, or /var/lib/kubelet into a container exposes node files and can be abused to escape or read node secrets.
items.how_to_find:
Find pods mounting sensitive host paths (`/`, `/etc`, `/var/run`, `/var/lib/kubelet`):

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.volumes[]?.hostPath) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name+" "+(.spec.volumes[]?.hostPath.path)'
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove sensitive `hostPath` mounts; use PVs/CSI volumes or projected volumes instead. If a path is truly required, mount it read-only and narrowly scoped. Block dangerous `hostPath` prefixes at admission with Kyverno/Gatekeeper.

items.title: Disable allowPrivilegeEscalation
items.description: Allowing privilege escalation lets a process gain more privileges than its parent (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Find containers allowing privilege escalation (setuid/`no_new_privs` off):

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.containers[] | (.securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation!=false)) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix:
Set `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` on every container (implied by `restricted` PSS):

```
securityContext: {allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, runAsNonRoot: true, capabilities: {drop: ["ALL"]}}
```

Enforce via Pod Security Admission `restricted`.

sections.title: Container Runtime Security — Linux Capabilities & SecurityContext

items.title: Drop excess Linux capabilities
items.description: Capabilities like CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_NET_RAW, and CAP_SYS_PTRACE grant powerful kernel-level operations that enable escape and lateral movement.
items.how_to_find:
Find containers that don't drop `ALL` capabilities or add dangerous ones (`NET_ADMIN`, `SYS_ADMIN`):

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.containers[] | (.securityContext.capabilities.drop // [] | index("ALL") | not)) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix:
Drop all capabilities and add back only what's required:

```
securityContext: {capabilities: {drop: ["ALL"], add: ["NET_BIND_SERVICE"]}}
```

Deny `SYS_ADMIN`/`NET_RAW` at admission via policy.

items.title: Define a securityContext for every workload
items.description: Workloads with no securityContext inherit permissive defaults — writable root filesystem, default capabilities, and no seccomp — maximizing post-exploitation options.
items.how_to_find:
Find pods/containers with no `securityContext` (defaults are permissive):

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.securityContext=={} and (.spec.containers[].securityContext==null)) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix:
Set a hardened context at pod and container level:

```
securityContext: {runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 1000, seccompProfile: {type: RuntimeDefault}, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, capabilities: {drop: ["ALL"]}}
```

Enforce the `restricted` PSS profile so unset contexts are rejected.

items.title: Apply a seccomp profile
items.description: Without seccomp, containers can issue any syscall, broadening the kernel attack surface available for container escape exploits.
items.how_to_find:
Find containers not using a seccomp profile (syscalls unrestricted):

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select((.spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type // .spec.containers[].securityContext.seccompProfile.type)==null) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix: Set `seccompProfile: {type: RuntimeDefault}` on workloads (default in PSS `restricted`). For sensitive apps, generate a tailored profile and load it as a `Localhost` profile. Enforce via admission policy.

items.title: Enforce read-only root filesystem
items.description: A writable root filesystem lets an attacker drop tools, modify binaries, and persist inside a compromised container.
items.how_to_find:
Find containers with a writable root filesystem:

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.containers[] | (.securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem!=true)) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix:
Set `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` and provide `emptyDir` volumes for writable paths:

```
securityContext: {readOnlyRootFilesystem: true}
volumeMounts: [{name: tmp, mountPath: /tmp}]
volumes: [{name: tmp, emptyDir: {}}]
```

Enforce at admission.

sections.title: Container Runtime Security — Container Escape Vectors

items.title: Patch runtime CVEs enabling escape (e.g. runc CVE-2024-21626)
items.description: Container runtime vulnerabilities such as the runc file-descriptor leak (CVE-2024-21626) and CVE-2019-5736 allow breakout to the host from a crafted image or workload.
items.how_to_find:
Check container runtime versions on nodes against known escape CVEs (runc/containerd):

```
kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.status.nodeInfo.containerRuntimeVersion}{"\n"}{end}' | sort -u
```

Vulnerable runc/containerd versions (e.g. runc < 1.1.12 for CVE-2024-21626) are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Patch containerd/runc on all nodes (or roll new node images) and drain/reboot:

```
kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
# upgrade node image / package, then:
kubectl uncordon <node>
```

Automate node image rotation and monitor CVE feeds.

items.title: No container engine socket mounted into pods
items.description: Mounting /var/run/docker.sock or the containerd socket gives a container full control of the engine, trivially launching privileged containers on the host.
items.how_to_find:
Detect socket mounts that grant node control:

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.volumes[]?.hostPath.path | test("\\.sock$")?) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove socket mounts; use rootless build tooling (Kaniko/Buildah) for CI. Block `*.sock` hostPath mounts at admission via Kyverno/Gatekeeper.

items.title: Test escape-to-node and node credential theft
items.description: A foothold on a node can yield kubelet credentials, mounted service-account tokens, and cloud instance metadata, enabling cluster-wide compromise.
items.how_to_find:
From a test pod, attempt to reach the node and steal credentials (authorized testing only):

```
kubectl run t --rm -it --image=alpine --overrides='{"spec":{"hostPID":true}}' -- sh
# inside: check for kubelet creds, node token, metadata
curl -s http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
cat /var/lib/kubelet/*/token 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce `restricted` PSS (no hostPID/privileged), block the metadata endpoint via NetworkPolicy, use node IMDSv2 with hop-limit 1, and scope node IAM roles minimally. Re-test that escape paths are closed.

items.title: Block kernel module loading and dangerous mounts from containers
items.description: Capabilities or device access that permit loading kernel modules or mounting filesystems give a direct route to host kernel compromise.
items.how_to_find:
Look for containers with `SYS_MODULE`/`SYS_ADMIN` or that can mount filesystems:

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.containers[].securityContext.capabilities.add[]? | test("SYS_MODULE|SYS_ADMIN")) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix: Drop `SYS_MODULE`/`SYS_ADMIN`, keep `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`, apply `RuntimeDefault` seccomp (blocks `mount`/`init_module`), and deny these capabilities at admission.

sections.title: Container Runtime Security — Running as Root

items.title: Require runAsNonRoot
items.description: Containers that run as root inside the namespace make capability abuse and escapes far more damaging, often equating to root on the node.
items.how_to_find:
Find containers that may run as root:

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select((.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot // .spec.containers[].securityContext.runAsNonRoot)!=true) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix:
Set `runAsNonRoot: true` and a non-zero `runAsUser`:

```
securityContext: {runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 1000}
```

Enforce via PSS `restricted`; build images with a non-root `USER`.

items.title: Build images with a non-root USER
items.description: Even with runtime controls, images defaulting to USER root run as root unless explicitly overridden, and may be deployed without the override.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect the image's configured user:

```
docker inspect <image>:<tag> --format '{{.Config.User}}'
```

Empty or `0`/`root` = finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Add a non-root user in the Dockerfile:

```
RUN addgroup -S app && adduser -S app -G app
USER 1000
```

Ensure file permissions allow the non-root user to run. Verify `docker inspect` shows a non-root `User`.

items.title: Scope fsGroup and supplemental groups
items.description: Overly broad fsGroup or supplemental group settings can grant unintended access to shared volumes and host-backed storage.
items.how_to_find:
Review pods with broad `fsGroup`/`supplementalGroups` that widen file access:

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | {n:.metadata.name, fsGroup:.spec.securityContext.fsGroup, groups:.spec.securityContext.supplementalGroups}'
```
items.how_to_fix: Set `fsGroup` only when shared volume access is required, use the minimal group ID, and avoid privileged supplemental groups. Combine with `readOnlyRootFilesystem` and least-privilege volume permissions.

items.title: Confirm no-new-privileges is effective for root containers
items.description: Where root is unavoidable, the no-new-privileges flag still limits setuid-based escalation paths and should be verified as active.
items.how_to_find:
Verify `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` is set (maps to `no_new_privs`), especially where root can't be avoided:

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.containers[].securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation!=false) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix: Set `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` on all containers and drop setuid capabilities. Prefer non-root; if root is unavoidable, combine `no_new_privs` + dropped caps + `RuntimeDefault` seccomp. Enforce at admission.

items.title: Audit cluster-admin and wildcard role bindings
items.description: Subjects bound to cluster-admin or roles using wildcard verbs/resources effectively own the cluster, so any compromise of those subjects is catastrophic.
items.how_to_find:
List subjects bound to `cluster-admin` and roles using wildcards:

```
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.roleRef.name=="cluster-admin") | .subjects[]?.name'
kubectl get clusterroles -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.rules[]? | (.verbs|index("*")) or (.resources|index("*"))) | .metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix:
Remove unnecessary `cluster-admin` bindings and replace wildcard roles with explicit verbs/resources scoped per namespace:

```
kubectl delete clusterrolebinding <binding>
```

Grant admin only to break-glass accounts with MFA + audit. Review with `rbac-tool`/`kubectl-who-can`.

items.title: Restrict privilege-escalation verbs (escalate, bind, impersonate)
items.description: Verbs like escalate, bind, impersonate, and create on pods/secrets let a low-privileged subject grant itself or assume higher privileges.
items.how_to_find:
Find roles granting `escalate`, `bind`, or `impersonate` (RBAC-bypass primitives):

```
kubectl get clusterroles,roles -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.rules[]?.verbs[]? | test("escalate|bind|impersonate")) | .metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove these verbs from all but tightly-controlled platform roles. Never grant them to app service accounts. Enforce with a policy that denies creation of roles containing `escalate`/`bind`/`impersonate` for untrusted subjects.

items.title: Limit get/list access to Secrets
items.description: Broad get/list on Secrets across namespaces lets an attacker harvest credentials and service-account tokens for lateral movement.
items.how_to_find:
Find who can read Secrets (a common credential-theft path):

```
kubectl get clusterroles,roles -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.rules[]? | (.resources|index("secrets")) and (.verbs[]? | test("get|list|watch"))) | .metadata.name'
kubectl auth can-i list secrets --as=system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<sa> -n <ns>
```
items.how_to_fix: Scope Secret access to specific named secrets per namespace, remove broad `list secrets` grants, and prefer the Secrets Store CSI driver so pods never call the Secrets API. Audit regularly.

items.title: Prefer namespaced Roles over ClusterRoles
items.description: Using ClusterRoles where a namespaced Role would suffice grants cluster-wide reach and breaks blast-radius containment.
items.how_to_find:
Identify ClusterRoleBindings that could be namespaced RoleBindings:

```
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.roleRef.kind=="ClusterRole") | .metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix: Convert cluster-wide grants to namespaced Roles + RoleBindings so access doesn't leak across tenants. Reserve ClusterRoles for genuinely cluster-scoped needs, and review bindings in GitOps.

sections.title: Kubernetes RBAC & API — API Server & Kubelet Exposure

items.title: Disable anonymous API server authentication
items.description: Anonymous authentication on the API server can expose cluster information or actions to unauthenticated callers depending on RBAC bindings.
items.how_to_find:
Check the API server for `--anonymous-auth`:

```
kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o yaml | grep anonymous-auth
curl -k https://<api>:6443/api  # should be 401/403, not 200
```

`--anonymous-auth=true` (or unauthenticated `200`) is critical.
items.how_to_fix: Set `--anonymous-auth=false` on the API server (managed clusters handle this; for kubeadm edit `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`). Ensure RBAC is the authorization mode. Re-test that unauthenticated calls return `401/403`.

items.title: Require authentication and authorization on the kubelet API
items.description: A kubelet with anonymous auth enabled and authorization mode AlwaysAllow lets anyone reaching port 10250 run commands in pods and read logs.
items.how_to_find:
Probe the kubelet read/write ports for anonymous access:

```
curl -sk https://<node>:10250/pods
curl -s http://<node>:10255/pods
```

A `pods` listing without auth (or `--authorization-mode=AlwaysAllow`) is critical (RCE on pods).
items.how_to_fix: Set kubelet `--anonymous-auth=false`, `--authorization-mode=Webhook`, and disable the read-only port (`--read-only-port=0`). Restrict `:10250` to the control plane via firewall/SG. Re-test that requests require auth.

items.title: Restrict network exposure of the API server
items.description: An internet-reachable API server multiplies the risk of credential brute force, known CVEs, and misconfiguration exploitation.
items.how_to_find:
Confirm `:6443` is not internet-reachable:

```
nmap -p 6443 --open <api-endpoint>
aws eks describe-cluster --name <c> --query 'cluster.resourcesVpcConfig.[endpointPublicAccess,publicAccessCidrs]'
```

Public + `0.0.0.0/0` = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Make the endpoint private or restrict `publicAccessCidrs` to admin IPs; access via VPN/bastion. On EKS use `endpointPublicAccess=false,endpointPrivateAccess=true`. Add firewall rules limiting `:6443`. Re-scan.

items.title: Verify API server audit logging is enabled
items.description: Without audit logging, attacker API activity such as secret reads, exec, and RBAC changes goes undetected and uninvestigable.
items.how_to_find:
Check for an audit policy + log backend on the API server:

```
kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o yaml | grep -E 'audit-log-path|audit-policy-file'
```

No audit config = critical (no forensic trail). Managed clusters: confirm control-plane logging is on (e.g. EKS `audit` log type).
items.how_to_fix:
Enable audit logging with a policy capturing metadata for all requests + request bodies for sensitive resources, ship to a SIEM:

```
--audit-policy-file=/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml --audit-log-path=/var/log/k8s-audit.log --audit-log-maxage=30
```

On EKS: `aws eks update-cluster-config --name <c> --logging '{"clusterLogging":[{"types":["audit"],"enabled":true}]}'`.

sections.title: Kubernetes RBAC & API — Service Accounts & Tokens

items.title: Disable automatic service-account token mounting where unused
items.description: By default a service-account token is mounted into every pod; if the workload does not call the API, this token is needless attack surface for a compromised container.
items.how_to_find:
Find pods/SAs that auto-mount a token they don't need:

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.automountServiceAccountToken!=false) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix:
Set `automountServiceAccountToken: false` on the SA or pod spec where the API isn't used:

```
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata: {name: <sa>, namespace: <ns>}
automountServiceAccountToken: false
```

This removes a token an attacker could steal from a compromised pod.

items.title: Ensure the default service account has no granted permissions
items.description: Workloads that do not specify a service account use the namespace default; any RBAC granted to it is inherited broadly and often unintentionally.
items.how_to_find:
Check bindings referencing the `default` SA in each namespace:

```
kubectl get rolebindings,clusterrolebindings -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.subjects[]?.name=="default") | .metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove any RoleBindings to `default` SAs, set `automountServiceAccountToken: false` on them, and give each workload its own least-privilege SA. The default SA should be effectively powerless.

items.title: Test for service-account token abuse from a compromised pod
items.description: A mounted token plus permissive RBAC lets an attacker enumerate and act on the API, often reaching secrets, exec, or privilege escalation.
items.how_to_find:
Simulate token theft and see what it grants (authorized testing):

```
kubectl exec -it <pod> -n <ns> -- cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
TOKEN=<...>; kubectl auth can-i --list --token="$TOKEN"
```

Broad permissions from a workload token = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Scope each SA minimally, disable token automount where unused, use bound/projected short-lived tokens, and add NetworkPolicy so pods can't freely reach the API server. Re-test that a stolen token grants little.

items.title: Use bound, short-lived projected tokens
items.description: Legacy long-lived secret-based tokens never expire and are highly valuable if leaked; bound projected tokens are audience-scoped and time-limited.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether SA tokens are legacy (non-expiring Secret-based) vs projected bound tokens:

```
kubectl get secrets -A --field-selector type=kubernetes.io/service-account-token
```

Long-lived Secret tokens are findings on modern clusters.
items.how_to_fix:
Use projected `serviceAccountToken` volumes with an audience + expiration:

```
volumes: [{name: tok, projected: {sources: [{serviceAccountToken: {audience: <aud>, expirationSeconds: 3600, path: token}}]}}]
```

Delete legacy token Secrets. Tokens then auto-rotate and are audience-bound.

items.title: Apply default-deny network policies
items.description: By default all pods can talk to all other pods; without a default-deny baseline, a single compromised pod can reach every service in the cluster.
items.how_to_find:
List namespaces lacking a default-deny policy:

```
for ns in $(kubectl get ns -o name | cut -d/ -f2); do echo "$ns: $(kubectl get netpol -n $ns --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l)"; done
```
items.how_to_fix:
Apply default-deny ingress+egress per namespace, then allow required flows explicitly:

```
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata: {name: default-deny, namespace: <ns>}
spec: {podSelector: {}, policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]}
```

Verify the CNI enforces it (Calico/Cilium).

items.title: Restrict egress to limit exfiltration and C2
items.description: Unrestricted egress lets a compromised pod reach the internet for command-and-control, data exfiltration, and pulling additional tooling.
items.how_to_find:
Check for egress policies; unrestricted egress lets a compromised pod exfiltrate/beacon:

```
kubectl get netpol -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.policyTypes|index("Egress")|not) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix:
Add egress policies allowing only required destinations (DNS, specific services), deny the rest:

```
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Egress]
  egress: [{to: [{namespaceSelector: {matchLabels: {kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system}}}], ports: [{protocol: UDP, port: 53}]}]
```

Consider Cilium/FQDN policies for domain-based egress control.

items.title: Block pod access to the cloud metadata endpoint
items.description: Reachable cloud metadata (169.254.169.254) from pods enables theft of node IAM credentials and SSRF-to-cloud escalation.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether pods can reach `169.254.169.254` (node credential theft path):

```
kubectl run t --rm -it --image=alpine -- sh -c 'wget -qO- http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ || echo blocked'
```

A metadata response = critical.
items.how_to_fix:
Block the metadata CIDR with a NetworkPolicy egress deny and enforce IMDSv2 with hop-limit 1 on nodes:

```
egress: [{to: [{ipBlock: {cidr: 0.0.0.0/0, except: [169.254.169.254/32]}}]}]
```

Use IRSA/Workload Identity so pods get scoped creds without the node role. Re-test that metadata is blocked.

items.title: Confirm the CNI actually enforces NetworkPolicy
items.description: Some CNI plugins ignore NetworkPolicy objects, so policies appear configured but provide no real isolation — a dangerous false sense of security.
items.how_to_find:
Some CNIs ignore NetworkPolicy. Apply a deny policy and test connectivity:

```
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -o wide | grep -Ei 'calico|cilium|weave|flannel'
kubectl exec <podA> -- curl -m3 <podB-ip>:<port>   # should fail under default-deny
```

If traffic still flows under default-deny, the CNI isn't enforcing = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Run a policy-enforcing CNI (Calico/Cilium) and validate with a test suite. If on flannel (no enforcement), migrate or add Calico for policy. Re-run the connectivity test to confirm deny works.

items.title: Enable encryption at rest for Secrets in etcd
items.description: By default Kubernetes Secrets are only base64-encoded in etcd; without encryption-at-rest, anyone reading etcd or its backups recovers all secrets in plaintext.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether Secrets are encrypted at rest (otherwise they're base64, not encrypted):

```
kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o yaml | grep encryption-provider-config
```

Missing `--encryption-provider-config` (self-managed) = high finding. Managed clusters: confirm KMS envelope encryption is enabled.
items.how_to_fix:
Configure an `EncryptionConfiguration` with a KMS provider and re-encrypt existing secrets:

```
--encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml
kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f -
```

On EKS: `aws eks associate-encryption-config --cluster-name <c> --encryption-config '[{"resources":["secrets"],"provider":{"keyArn":"<cmk>"}}]'`.

items.title: Avoid injecting secrets as environment variables
items.description: Secrets in environment variables leak via crash dumps, child-process inheritance, /proc, logs, and debug endpoints more readily than mounted files.
items.how_to_find:
Find secrets exposed via env (leak via `/proc`, logs, crash dumps):

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.containers[].env[]?.valueFrom.secretKeyRef) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix: Mount secrets as files (tmpfs) or via the Secrets Store CSI driver rather than env vars. Update apps to read from the mounted path and rotate any leaked values.

items.title: Restrict permissions on mounted secret files
items.description: Secret volumes mounted with overly permissive file modes can be read by unexpected processes or sidecars within the pod.
items.how_to_find:
Check `defaultMode` on secret volumes (world/group-readable files leak to sidecars):

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | .spec.volumes[]? | select(.secret) | .secret.defaultMode'
```

Modes broader than `0400`/`0440` are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Set restrictive file modes and run as non-root:

```
volumes: [{name: sec, secret: {secretName: <s>, defaultMode: 0400}}]
```

Combine with `readOnlyRootFilesystem` and minimal `fsGroup` so only the intended process can read the secret.

items.title: Prefer an external secrets manager with rotation
items.description: Native Secrets lack first-class rotation and fine-grained audit; integrating an external manager reduces standing exposure and improves traceability.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether secrets live only in etcd (static) vs an external manager with rotation:

```
kubectl get secrets -A --no-headers | wc -l
kubectl get externalsecrets -A 2>/dev/null
```

No external manager / no rotation = critical for sensitive credentials.
items.how_to_fix:
Adopt External Secrets Operator or the Secrets Store CSI driver backed by Vault/ASM/GSM with automatic rotation:

```
kubectl apply -f externalsecret.yaml   # syncs from the manager on an interval
```

Enable rotation on the backend and remove long-lived static secrets.

items.title: Enforce TLS on ingress and internal traffic
items.description: Ingress without TLS exposes credentials and tokens to interception, and plaintext east-west traffic enables sniffing after a foothold.
items.how_to_find:
Find ingresses without TLS and services serving plaintext:

```
kubectl get ingress -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.tls==null) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```

items.title: Identify unintentionally exposed Services (LoadBalancer/NodePort)
items.description: LoadBalancer and NodePort services can expose internal applications, dashboards, and databases directly to the internet.
items.how_to_find:
List Services exposed beyond the cluster:

```
kubectl get svc -A --field-selector spec.type=LoadBalancer
kubectl get svc -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.type=="NodePort") | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```

Then confirm reachability with `nmap` against the external IP/nodes.
items.how_to_fix:
Switch internal services to `ClusterIP`, front intended exposure through a single hardened ingress, and restrict LoadBalancer source ranges:

```
spec: {loadBalancerSourceRanges: [<admin-cidr>]}
```

Remove NodePorts that aren't required and add NetworkPolicy.

items.title: Check for exposed Kubernetes Dashboard and admin UIs
items.description: An exposed or weakly protected Kubernetes Dashboard (or app admin panel) has historically led directly to full cluster compromise (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Look for the Dashboard or other admin UIs exposed without auth:

```
kubectl get svc -A | grep -Ei 'dashboard|kubernetes-dashboard'
curl -sk https://<exposed-ip>/  # check for unauthenticated Dashboard
```

An internet-reachable Dashboard (or one with a privileged SA) is critical.
items.how_to_fix: Do not expose the Dashboard publicly; access via `kubectl proxy` only. If required, put it behind SSO/auth proxy, give it a minimal SA, and restrict with NetworkPolicy. Remove any `cluster-admin` binding for the Dashboard SA.

items.title: Review ingress controller annotations for injection risks
items.description: Ingress controllers like ingress-nginx have had vulnerabilities (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Audit ingress annotations for risky snippets (nginx `configuration-snippet`/`server-snippet` enabling CRLF/config injection, e.g. CVE-2021-25742):

```
kubectl get ingress -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.metadata.annotations | keys[]? | test("snippet")) | .metadata.namespace+"/"+.metadata.name'
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable snippet annotations in the ingress-nginx ConfigMap (`allow-snippet-annotations: "false"`), keep the controller patched, and restrict who can create/edit Ingress objects via RBAC. Validate annotations in CI/admission.

sections.title: Cluster Hardening & Supply Chain — Admission Control & Policy

items.title: Enforce Pod Security Admission (restricted) on namespaces
items.description: PodSecurityPolicy is removed; without Pod Security Admission or an equivalent, nothing prevents privileged, host-sharing, or root workloads from being created.
items.how_to_find:
Check PSA enforce labels per namespace:

```
kubectl get ns -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.metadata.labels.pod-security\.kubernetes\.io/enforce}{"\n"}{end}'
```

Namespaces without `enforce=restricted` are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Label namespaces `enforce=restricted` (roll out via `warn`/`audit` first):

```
kubectl label ns <ns> pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted --overwrite
```

Manage labels in GitOps so they can't drift.

items.title: Deploy a policy engine (OPA/Gatekeeper or Kyverno)
items.description: A policy engine enforces organization-specific guardrails — trusted registries, no latest tags, required labels, blocked capabilities — beyond what PSA covers.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether an admission policy engine is installed and enforcing:

```
kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations
kubectl get clusterpolicies 2>/dev/null; kubectl get constrainttemplates 2>/dev/null
```

None = finding (no guardrails beyond PSS).
items.how_to_fix:
Install Kyverno or Gatekeeper and deploy enforcing policies (trusted registries, no privileged, require limits, disallow hostPath):

```
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno/releases/latest/download/install.yaml
kubectl apply -f policies/   # validationFailureAction: Enforce
```

Test policies in audit mode first, then enforce.

items.title: Harden admission webhook configuration
items.description: Misconfigured admission webhooks (failurePolicy: Ignore, overly broad rules, or a compromised webhook) can be bypassed or abused to weaken or block cluster controls.
items.how_to_find:
Review webhook configs for risky settings (`failurePolicy: Ignore`, overly broad rules, no TLS/caBundle):

```
kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations,mutatingwebhookconfigurations -o json | jq -r '.items[] | .webhooks[] | {name, failurePolicy, sideEffects}'
```
items.how_to_fix: Set `failurePolicy: Fail` for security-critical webhooks, scope `rules`/`namespaceSelector` tightly (exclude `kube-system` to avoid lockout), require valid `caBundle` + TLS, and set `timeoutSeconds` sensibly. Monitor webhook availability.

items.title: Enforce supply-chain policy at admission (signing/provenance)
items.description: Admission-time verification of image signatures and provenance attestations stops untrusted or tampered images from ever running.
items.how_to_find:
Check for image-verification policies at admission:

```
kubectl get clusterpolicies -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.rules[]?.verifyImages) | .metadata.name'
```

None = critical (unsigned/untrusted images can run).
items.how_to_fix:
Enforce signature + provenance verification via Kyverno `verifyImages` (or Sigstore policy-controller):

```
rules: [{name: verify, verifyImages: [{imageReferences: ["<registry>/*"], attestors: [{entries: [{keys: {publicKeys: "<key>"}}]}]}]}]
```

Deny images that aren't signed by trusted keys/identities and lack SLSA provenance.

sections.title: Cluster Hardening & Supply Chain — etcd Security

items.title: Restrict network exposure of etcd
items.description: etcd reachable beyond the control plane allows direct reads/writes of all cluster state and secrets, bypassing the API server and RBAC entirely.
items.how_to_find:
Confirm etcd ports (`2379`/`2380`) aren't reachable beyond the control plane:

```
nmap -p 2379,2380 --open <node>
curl -k https://<node>:2379/version   # should require client certs
```

Any broader reachability = finding (etcd holds all cluster secrets).
items.how_to_fix: Bind etcd to the control-plane network only, firewall `2379/2380` to control-plane nodes, and require client certs. Managed clusters handle this. Re-scan to confirm no external reachability.

items.title: Require mutual TLS for etcd client and peer traffic
items.description: Without client-cert authentication and peer TLS, anyone with network access to etcd can read or modify cluster data without credentials.
items.how_to_find:
Check etcd flags for TLS + client cert auth:

```
kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=etcd -o yaml | grep -E 'cert-file|key-file|client-cert-auth|peer-.*tls'
```

Missing `--client-cert-auth=true` / peer TLS = critical.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable mTLS for client and peer traffic:

```
--cert-file=... --key-file=... --client-cert-auth=true --trusted-ca-file=...
--peer-client-cert-auth=true --peer-cert-file=... --peer-key-file=...
```

Rotate certs regularly. On managed clusters this is enforced by the provider.

items.title: Protect and encrypt etcd backups
items.description: etcd snapshots contain every secret and config in the cluster; unencrypted or world-readable backups are a complete cluster breach if obtained.
items.how_to_find:
Check how etcd snapshots are stored (unencrypted snapshots leak all secrets):

```
etcdctl snapshot status <snapshot.db>
ls -l /var/lib/etcd/backups 2>/dev/null
```

Unencrypted / world-readable / off-box-in-clear backups are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Encrypt snapshots at rest (KMS/SSE), restrict access, and store off-cluster with integrity checks:

```
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save snap.db --cacert=... --cert=... --key=...
gpg -c snap.db   # or upload to KMS-encrypted bucket
```

Test restores and apply retention.

items.title: Confirm data-at-rest encryption covers etcd contents
items.description: Even with network controls, plaintext secrets in the etcd datastore are exposed to disk theft, snapshot leaks, and host compromise.
items.how_to_find:
Verify that Secrets (and ideally all resources) are encrypted in etcd, not just base64:

```
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/<ns>/<name> --cacert=... --cert=... --key=... | hexdump -C | head
```

Readable plaintext = high finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable `EncryptionConfiguration` with a KMS provider covering `secrets` (and sensitive CRDs), then rewrite existing objects:

```
kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f -
```

Confirm the raw etcd value is now ciphertext.

sections.title: Cluster Hardening & Supply Chain — CIS Benchmark & Control-Plane Hardening

items.title: Run kube-bench against control plane and nodes
items.description: The CIS Kubernetes Benchmark codifies hundreds of hardening checks; running kube-bench surfaces insecure flags and file permissions that manual review misses.
items.how_to_find:
Run the CIS Kubernetes Benchmark:

```
kube-bench run --targets master,node,etcd,policies
```

`[FAIL]` items are findings; focus on control-plane and kubelet checks first.
items.how_to_fix: Remediate FAIL items (flag/permission fixes), re-run kube-bench, and add it to CI/scheduled scans. Track exceptions with justification. Managed clusters: focus on node/policy sections you control.

items.title: Harden API server flags
items.description: Insecure API server flags (e.g. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
Review API server flags against CIS (anonymous-auth, authorization-mode, admission plugins, TLS):

```
kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o yaml | grep -E '\-\-(anonymous-auth|authorization-mode|enable-admission-plugins|tls-cipher|audit-log)'
```
items.how_to_fix: Set: `--anonymous-auth=false`, `--authorization-mode=Node,RBAC`, `--enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,PodSecurity`, strong `--tls-cipher-suites`, and audit logging. Apply via the static manifest (self-managed) and re-run kube-bench.

items.title: Harden kubelet configuration
items.description: Kubelet defaults can permit anonymous access, read-only port exposure, and unrotated certs, providing node and pod control to attackers.
items.how_to_find:
Review kubelet config against CIS:

```
kubectl get --raw "/api/v1/nodes/<node>/proxy/configz" | jq '.kubeletconfig | {authentication, authorization, readOnlyPort, protectKernelDefaults}'
```

`readOnlyPort!=0`, anonymous auth on, or `AlwaysAllow` are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Set `authentication.anonymous.enabled=false`, `authorization.mode=Webhook`, `readOnlyPort=0`, `protectKernelDefaults=true`, and rotate kubelet certs. Apply via kubelet config file and restart; re-run kube-bench.

items.title: Harden the node OS and component file permissions
items.description: Insecure permissions on kubeconfig, manifests, and PKI files, plus an unhardened host OS, let a node-local attacker read credentials and tamper with components.
items.how_to_find:
Check permissions/ownership of control-plane files and kubeconfigs (world-readable keys are findings):

```
kube-bench run --targets node
ls -l /etc/kubernetes/manifests /etc/kubernetes/pki /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
```
items.how_to_fix: Set `600`/`644` per CIS on manifests, PKI, and kubeconfigs; `root:root` ownership. Harden the node OS (CIS-hardened image, minimal packages, auto-patching), disable SSH where possible, and use read-only/immutable node images. Re-run kube-bench.
